anchorx="margin"/></v:rect></w:pict></mc:Fallback></mc:AlternateContent></w:r><w:r><w:t>The project will be following Waterfall Methodology, which is step-by-step and structured development approach. The waterfall approach is a linear and sequential process, in which the predecessor phase must be completed before moving onto the next phase of the project. Since the requirements for the project are simple, clear and fixed so the waterfall approach is a good fit for smooth workflow as well as organized development of the system and timely scheduled completion of each phase without unnecessary frequent changes.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="503A6087" w14:textId="09261845" w:rsidR="00241393" w:rsidRDefault="00241393" w:rsidP="00241393"/><w:p Background Study</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="19"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="2D3406E3" w14:textId="5A075CD0" w:rsidR="008409A7" w:rsidRPr="008409A7" w:rsidRDefault="008409A7" w:rsidP="000E5291"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:szCs w:val="32"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Blood donation plays a vital role in healthcare systems, providing essential support during medical emergencies, surgeries, and treatments for chronic illnesses. However, many regions still rely on traditional, manual methods to manage blood requests and locate suitable donors. These methods often involve personal communication, social media appeals, or outdated records, which can lead to delays, mismatched blood groups, and difficulty verifying donor eligibility. With increasing demand for timely blood availability, the need for a modern, automated, and reliable system has become more evident.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="3FF90DA7" w14:textId="1A2C1DC4" w:rsidR="008409A7" w:rsidRDefault="008409A7" w:rsidP="000E5291"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="NormalWeb"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">Recent technological advancements have enabled the use of intelligent systems and machine learning techniques to improve healthcare processes. </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>SmartBlood</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> Connect is developed with this perspective, integrating automation, prediction, and real-time donor matching. By utilizing the </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00F200B0"><w:t>Logistic Regression</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> algorithm, the system evaluates donor attributes such as age, location, medical conditions, and past donation patterns to estimate their likelihood of donating. Additionally, the system incorporates a mapping algorithm to locate the nearest eligible donor, ensuring faster response during critical situations.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="78CB693D" w14:textId="634C9FB5" w:rsidR="00A16ED4" w:rsidRDefault="008409A7" w:rsidP="000E5291"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="NormalWeb"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">This background highlights the importance of transitioning from manual procedures to a data-driven, centralized platform that enhances coordination between patients, donors, and admins. </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>SmartBlood</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> Connect aims to address existing limitations and support healthcare systems by providing improved accuracy, efficiency, and accessibility in blood donation management.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="79EE290F" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="000E5291" w:rsidRDefault="00A16ED4" w:rsidP="000E5291"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading2"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="20" w:name="_Toc216505507"/><w:r><w:t>2.2 Literature Review</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="20"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="58DAB69E" w14:textId="64285267" w:rsidR="000E5291" w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1" w:rsidRDefault="000E5291" w:rsidP="000E5291"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:szCs w:val="32"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">Several researchers have explored the integration of intelligent algorithms in healthcare and blood-donation management systems. R. Menon (2020)</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> proposed the </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Emphasis"/><w:rFonts w:eastAsiaTheme="majorEastAsia"/></w:rPr><w:t>DonorMatch</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Emphasis"/><w:rFonts w:eastAsiaTheme="majorEastAsia"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> Algorithm</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:t>, a rule-based filtering approach that identifies donors based on blood group compatibility and medical history. Yadav (2018)</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> introduced the </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Emphasis"/><w:rFonts w:eastAsiaTheme="majorEastAsia"/></w:rPr><w:t>GeoHealth</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Emphasis"/><w:rFonts w:eastAsiaTheme="majorEastAsia"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> Mapping Algorithm</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:t xml:space="preserve">, which used location clustering to find nearby medical resources but was not specifically designed for blood donation. feasibility study is an assessment of the project that determines the likelihood of a project. It analyzes the viability of projects to determine whether the project will succeed or not. It evaluates all the necessary criteria that are important for the completion of the project with great outcome. Some of the feasibility required for the project are described below:</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="7BB4420A" w14:textId="11DF419F" w:rsidR="00DB6031" w:rsidRDefault="00C07302" w:rsidP="00C07302"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading4"/><w:numPr><w:ilvl w:val="0"/><w:numId w:val="8"/></w:numPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:ind w:left="220" w:hanging="220"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Technical Feasibility</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="6EF12277" w14:textId="26E8C53F" w:rsidR="00C612DD" w:rsidRDefault="00C612DD" w:rsidP="007232A0"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">The project is technically feasible to build because the system uses reliable and widely supported technologies. For predicting donor likelihood, </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00F200B0" w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Strong"/><w:b w:val="0"/></w:rPr><w:t>Logistic Regression</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Strong"/><w:b w:val="0"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> algorithm</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> is used, and for finding the nearest donors, </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00F200B0" w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Strong"/><w:b w:val="0"/></w:rPr><w:t>Random Forest</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Strong"/><w:b w:val="0"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> algorithm</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> is integrated with mapping. From the website perspective, the system </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:t xml:space="preserve">uses </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="007232A0" w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:t>HTML,</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="007232A0" w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="007232A0" w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Strong"/><w:b w:val="0"/></w:rPr><w:t>CSS,</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00F84CB1"><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="Strong"/><w:b w:val="0"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> Python, and Django</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> – these tools are easy to use, open-source, and commonly used in real-world projects, making the system easy to build and maintain.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="14C08156" w14:textId="133B8F32" w:rsidR="00C07302" w:rsidRDefault="00C07302" w:rsidP="00C07302"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading4"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>ii Operational Feasibility</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="0F04E7BC" w14:textId="38F668C3" w:rsidR="00C07302" w:rsidRDefault="007232A0" w:rsidP="00754444"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Technologies for managing blood donations and requests are widely used and growing, and this project meets the needs of users. </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00560405"><w:t>Blood Bank Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>system</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">, which illustrates how the main classes are related in a particular scenario. The model includes key objects such as </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>UserProfile</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">, Patient, Donor, Admin, </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>DonorLocation</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">, </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>BloodRequest</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">, </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>BloodInventory</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">, </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>BloodIssuance</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">, </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>DonorNotification</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">, and </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>DonationAppointment</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">. Each object instance carries its own attribute values; for example, a Donor object holds details like blood type, weight, last donation date, and current eligibility status, while a </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>BloodRequest</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> object stores the required blood type, number of units, hospital location, and urgency. In the same way, every object in the diagram reflects the concrete data held by its corresponding class at runtime.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="3D4E6A63" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00DF5F16" w:rsidRDefault="00DF5F16" w:rsidP="002A1D15"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>The relationships between these objects describe how they work together for one blood</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t xml:space="preserve">request case. A single </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>UserProfile</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> object is linked to either a Patient, Donor, or Admin object to represent the role of that user in the system. The Patient object is connected to a </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>BloodRequest</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> object, which captures a specific demand for blood units. That </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>BloodRequest</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> is associated with one or more </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>DonorNotification</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> objects sent to different Donor objects who match the required blood type and location, and may also be linked to a </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>DonationAppointment</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> when a donor agrees to donate. On the inventory side, each </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>BloodInventory</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> object is tied to a Donor object that provided the unit, and to a </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>BloodIssuance</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> object when that unit is finally issued to the Patient to fulfill the request. This object diagram therefore presents a snapshot of how actual instances of patients, donors, requests, notifications, appointments, and blood units are connected to deliver a complete blood</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>matching and transfusion workflow in the system.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="522A92EC" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00DF5F16" Dynamic Modeling: State and Sequence Diagram</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="30"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="5C7CC77B" w14:textId="176F671A" w:rsidR="0035416E" w:rsidRPr="00D06BA0" w:rsidRDefault="0035416E" w:rsidP="0035416E"><w:pPr><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00D06BA0"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/></w:rPr><w:t>State Diagram</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00D06BA0" w:rsidRPr="00D06BA0"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/></w:rPr><w:t>:</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="7645E081" w14:textId="1D0655B1" w:rsidR="002D632E" w:rsidRDefault="002D632E" w:rsidP="002D632E"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">The state diagram illustrates the overall interaction flow of </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00560405"><w:t>Blood Bank Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> from a user’s point of view. The process starts at the initial state, where a new user must first register to gain access to the system. After successful registration, the user is directed to the login state and enters their credentials to authenticate. If the login attempt is valid, the user is moved to the main user home state; if the credentials are invalid, an error is shown and the user is returned to the login state to try again.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="71C2BB82" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="002D632E" w:rsidRDefault="002D632E" w:rsidP="002D632E"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>From the user home state, the behavior branches according to the role of the logged</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>in account. In the patient path, the user can create a new blood request, enter details such as hospital, blood type, required units, and urgency, and then submit the request. The system then checks the blood inventory; if enough compatible units are available, units are reserved or issued and the request status is updated, while a lack of stock triggers a search for suitable donors and the sending of donor notifications. Patients can also move to states for viewing request history and monitoring fulfillment progress. In the donor path, the user can update profile and location information, view nearby requests and notifications, decide whether they are willing to donate, schedule a donation appointment, and complete a donation so that a new blood unit is added to inventory. Common states, accessible to both roles, include viewing the dashboard, checking overall blood inventory summaries, and reviewing notifications. Eventually, the user transitions to the logout state, which ends the active session and leads to the final terminal state.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="5BA8DDC5" w14:textId="710CD1FB" w:rsidR="0051322A" w:rsidRDefault="0051322A" w:rsidP="00EB6EFC"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="1E245F46" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="0035416E" w:rsidRDefault="0035416E" w:rsidP="00EB6EFC"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="4AB0FE67" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="0035416E" w:rsidRDefault="0035416E" w:rsidP="00EB6EFC"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="7C068DBF" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t xml:space="preserve">The Admin UI forwards this action to the Issuance Service together with the chosen blood unit, the associated request, and the patient. The service then creates a new </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t>BloodIssuance</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> record in the database to log which unit is being transfused for which request. Once the issuance is saved, the Issuance Service updates the corresponding </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t>BloodInventory</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> entry by changing its status to issued, increments the </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t>units_fulfilled</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> field of the </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t>BloodRequest</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t xml:space="preserve">, and increases the patient’s </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t>total_units_received</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="006B3219"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> statistic. Finally, the service sends an issuance result back to the Admin UI, which refreshes the request view so the admin can see the updated fulfillment status and confirm that the blood unit has been successfully issued.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="48446FDF" w14:textId="5E92997F" w:rsidR="006B3219" w:rsidRDefault="006B3219" w:rsidP="006B3219"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Caption"/><w:spacing w14:paraId="17FCDE91" w14:textId="69E92421" w:rsidR="00476300" w:rsidRPr="00DF51AE" w:rsidRDefault="00476300" w:rsidP="00476300"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Caption"/><w:spacing w:before="320"/><w:jc w:val="center"/><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:color w:val="auto"/><w:sz w:val="24"/><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00DF51AE"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:color w:val="auto"/><w:sz w:val="24"/><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve">Figure 3. </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="006178DF"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:color w:val="auto"/><w:sz w:val="24"/><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr><w:t>10</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00DF51AE"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:color w:val="auto"/><w:sz w:val="24"/><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> Activity Diagram of </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:color w:val="auto"/><w:sz w:val="24"/><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr><w:t>BloodBank</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:color w:val="auto"/><w:sz w:val="24"/><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00DF51AE"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:color w:val="auto"/><w:sz w:val="24"/><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> – Admin</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="77EB4682" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00476300" w:rsidRPr="00476300" w:rsidRDefault="00476300" w:rsidP="00476300"/><w:p w14:paraId="72C3919C" w14:textId="573E8BCC" w:rsidR="00BA61AC" w:rsidRPr="00BA61AC" w:rsidRDefault="008C226F" w:rsidP="00F84CB1"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:after="160" w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:br w:type="page"/></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00BA61AC" w:rsidRPr="00BA61AC"><w:t>The donor activity diagram shows the lifecycle of a donor session. It begins when a person registers as a donor and then logs into the system. Credentials are checked; if they are invalid, an error is displayed and the user is sent back to the login step. After successful authentication, the donor reaches the donor dashboard and can update their donor profile, manage multiple saved locations, and view nearby blood requests and notifications. When a relevant request appears, the donor decides whether they are willing to donate. If they accept, the system guides them through scheduling a donation appointment, donating blood at a collection center, and then viewing their donation history. If they decline or ignore the request, the flow bypasses the appointment and donation steps and moves directly toward logout, where the session ends.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="53B60AE0" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00BA61AC" w:rsidRPr="00BA61AC" w:rsidRDefault="00BA61AC" w:rsidP="00F84CB1"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00BA61AC"><w:t>The patient activity diagram focuses on how a patient interacts with the system. The process starts with registration and login, followed by a credential check that either opens the main dashboard or returns an error and loops back to the login state. From the dashboard, a patient can create a new blood request, specifying details about the needed units and hospital. The patient can then view the status of existing requests, search for available blood in the inventory, and review notifications related to their requests or scheduled donations. The flow also includes managing the patient profile and ends with the logout action, which terminates the current session.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="09CC214C" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00BA61AC" w:rsidRPr="00BA61AC" w:rsidRDefault="00BA61AC" w:rsidP="00F84CB1"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00BA61AC"><w:t>The admin activity diagram represents the management workflow for administrative users. After logging in and passing the credential check, the admin reaches the admin dashboard. From there, the admin can manage all user accounts, including patients, donors, and other admins. The admin is responsible for approving blood requests, searching and reserving inventory, issuing blood units for requests, and recording completed donations. Additional activities include monitoring inventory levels to ensure adequate stock and viewing analytical reports that summarize system usage and blood movement. As with the other roles, the admin’s session concludes with a logout state.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="455F88B2" w14:textId="706D1FD8" w:rsidR="006C0185" w:rsidRDefault="006C0185" w:rsidP="006C0185"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading2"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="37" w:name="_Toc216505515"/><w:r><w:t>3.2 System Design</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="37"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="0D89B6CA" w14:textId="093D7D03" w:rsidR="006C0185" w:rsidRDefault="006C0185" w:rsidP="006C0185"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading3"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="38" w:name="_Toc216505516"/><w:r><w:t>3.2.1 Refinement of Classes and Objects</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="38"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="2A3CAD97" w14:textId="7316E2FB" w:rsidR="00136C00" w:rsidRPr="00B75446" w:rsidRDefault="00136C00" w:rsidP="00B75446"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00B75446"><w:t xml:space="preserve">The refined class diagram summarizes the core structure of the </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00560405"><w:t>Blood Bank Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00B75446"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00B75446"><w:t>system</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00B75446"><w:t>. w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">The component diagram shows the relationship between different components in the application. The diagram basically explains and shows how each component interact with each other to perform different </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00DE5612"><w:t>functions such as training the model, providing analyzed videos, managing users, uploading videos and so on. In the diagram there are mainly 6 different components: Admin, User, Dataset, Event Detection, Analysis Model, Match Video. The rectangle represents the components. The semi -circle on a line represents required interface and full circle represents provided interface.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="347CA70A" w14:textId="7397C000" w:rsidR="00DE5612" w:rsidRDefault="00DE5612" w:rsidP="005C6D34"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">From the diagram we can figure out that admin manages the users and operates the uploaded match videos in User and Mach Video </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="002C75DD"><w:t>component,</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> respectively. The admin also provides different training models to perform and upgrade the analysis model. The analysis model requires dataset to train, validate and test. The analysis model requests the labelled data frames from the dataset component. The user requests for video analysis by uploading the video in the system and it uses analysis model to get the outcome. The user can also request add the videos to </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00FC364F"><w:t>favorite and</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> request additional features like downloading the video or generating the reports.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="14D37010" w14:textId="0CCE8F1C" w:rsidR="00CD2123" w:rsidRDefault="00476300" w:rsidP="005C6D34"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:rsidP="009E4159"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">The deployment diagram of </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidR="00660D6F"><w:t>BloodBank</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidR="00660D6F"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> Management</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="007435F9"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> System</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> represents relationship between different components and nodes of the system. The system consists of four important nodes: Database server node, Admin node, User node and Analysis Model server node. The database server node is connected to every node in the system, it is responsible for storing the details of users, admins and videos that exists in the system. The server node is responsible for processing all kinds of information provided by the users like uploading videos, signing in and out from the system, favoriting videos, downloading reports and videos. The server node is connected with database server node, user node and admin node with an association relationship with every node. </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00D458B8"><w:t>The admin node is generally responsible for controlling and managing the system environment. It is connected with server node and database node, the main priority of admin node is user management, video management and updating / training the new models / dataset for giving better accuracy result to the uploaded video by users. The user node is responsible for accessing the system and exploring multiple features like uploading videos for getting analyzed result, favorite the analyzed video, downloading the video and its report.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="3F5A353F" w14:textId="498497D5" w:rsidR="00D458B8" w:rsidRDefault="00476300" w:rsidP="00476300"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc System</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="43"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="7EC1A589" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00532749" w:rsidRPr="0053280E" w:rsidRDefault="00532749" w:rsidR="00D458B8" w:rsidRDefault="00F174D0" w:rsidP="00F174D0"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading2"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="44" w:name="_Toc216505519"/><w:r><w:t>3.3 Algorithm</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="44"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="48DBB620" w14:textId="5DD23767" w:rsidR="0053280E" w:rsidRPr="0053280E" w:rsidRDefault="00560405" w:rsidP="0053280E"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Blood Bank Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="0053280E" w:rsidRPr="0053280E"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> is a web</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="0053280E" w:rsidRPr="0053280E"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t xml:space="preserve">based application that predicts donor </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidR="0053280E" w:rsidRPr="0053280E"><w:t>behaviour</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidR="0053280E" w:rsidRPr="0053280E"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> to support blood request fulfillment. To keep the system efficient and scalable on modest hardware, two main algorithms are selected: Random Forest Classifier and Manual Logistic Regression. These algorithms are well suited to the project’s data size, interpretability needs, and deployment constraints, and together they provide a complete pipeline from initial donor response prediction to final donation outcome analysis. The models are trained on historical notification and donation records to accurately classify, analyze, and predict the events in the donor workflow.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="2FE6BA2D" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="0053280E" w:rsidRPr="0053280E" w:rsidRDefault="0053280E" w:rsidP="0053280E"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:b/><w:sz w:val="28"/><w:szCs w:val="28"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="0053280E"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:sz w:val="28"/><w:szCs w:val="28"/></w:rPr><w:t>1) Random Forest Classifier – Donor Response Prediction</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="7E8D3936" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>The Random Forest Classifier is the primary algorithm used to predict if a donor will respond to a notification. It is an ensemble of decision trees that combines many weak learners into a strong, robust classifier and handles non</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>linear relationships and mixed</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>scale features effectively.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="0AD8BD3D" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>How it Works?</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="37E080F0" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t>Step 1: Define Behavioral Features</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:br/><w:t>For each donor notification, the system converts the donor–request pair into a numeric feature vector. A </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>StandardScaler</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t> is applied on the input features Feature importance scores are computed to highlight which factors (for example, </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>distance_km</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t> or </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>urgency_score</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>) contribute most to the prediction.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="719B2A45" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t>Step 5: Save Trained Model for Production Use</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="5D3025B4" w14:textId="77F05ED5" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>Once the model is trained and evaluated, both the Random Forest model and the scaler, together with the feature name ordering, are stored in a single serialized file (</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>donor_response_model.pkl</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>). This file is saved inside the project so that the web application can load it later without retraining every time the server starts.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="5B7306CA" w14:textId="77777777" This allows reuse of the same trained parameters for later experiments or analysis without retraining.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="6A2121AE" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00823991" w:rsidRPr="00823991" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t>Step 4: Interpreting Probability as Donation Likelihood</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="209A9326" w14:textId="687BA2CB" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>To make the probabilities more understandable, a helper function maps the raw probability value to human</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>readable labels such as:</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="650BEA79" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>&lt; 0.2: “Very unlikely to donate soon”</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="152F23B6" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>0.2 – 0.5: “Low Likelihood”</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="4C79CB58" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>0.5 – 0.8: “Moderate Likelihood”</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="777ABAB8" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRPr="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>&gt; 0.8: “Very likely to donate again”</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="3A5A172F" w14:textId="0093304C" w:rsidR="00823991" w:rsidRDefault="009F5547" w:rsidP="009F5547"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="009F5547"><w:t>This interpretation is useful when presenting results in reports or admin dashboards.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="7BF4FCCD" w14:textId="766E8F50" w:rsidR="009F5547" w:rsidRDefault="00823991" w:rsidP="00823991"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:after="160" w:line="278" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:br w:type="page"/></w:r><w:bookmarkStart w:id="45" w:name="_GoBack"/><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="45"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="5E387312" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00823991" w:rsidRPr="00823991" w:rsidRDefault="00823991" w:rsidP="00823991"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t>The two algorithms work together in a staged prediction pipeline. Random Forest first predicts which donors are likely to </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:bdr w:val="single" w:sz="2" w:space="0" w:color="auto" w:frame="1"/></w:rPr><w:t>respond</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t> to notifications, prioritizing those with strong past response behavior, shorter distance to the hospital, and an exact blood</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>group match. Logistic Regression then estimates, among those responders, which donors are most likely to actually </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:rPr><w:b/><w:bCs/><w:bdr w:val="single" w:sz="2" w:space="0" w:color="auto" w:frame="1"/></w:rPr><w:t>complete</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t> the donation, providing hospitals with an interpretable probability (for example, 0.82 = very likely) to guide scheduling and allocation decisions.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="36A36211" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00823991" w:rsidRPr="00823991" w:rsidRDefault="00823991" w:rsidP="00823991"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t>For example, suppose there are 100 potential donors for an emergency O</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t xml:space="preserve">negative request. Random Forest might predict that only 25 of them have a high response probability based on features such as </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t>distance_km</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t xml:space="preserve">, </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t>days_since_last_donation</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t xml:space="preserve">, </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t>urgency_score</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> and </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t>response_rate</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:t>. From these 25, Logistic Regression could then rank donors by their donation completion probability: Donor A with 0.91, Donor B with 0.78, Donor C with 0.62, and so on. The system can first notify and call Donor A and B, while keeping others as backups, ensuring that limited time and communication efforts focus on donors who are both likely to respond and likely to show up. This two</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>step approach keeps the Blood Bank Management System lightweight and explainable while still using machine learning to support real</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>time, data</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00823991"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>driven decisions in blood bank management.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="5A0854C8" w14:textId="07C53284" w:rsidR="0053280E" w:rsidRPr="0053280E" w:rsidRDefault="002E0F45" w:rsidP="002E0F45"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:after="160" w:line="278" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:br w:type="page"/></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="2D21E2F4" w14:textId="296B3313" w:rsidR="008F349C" w:rsidRDefault="00D10183" w:rsidP="00D10183"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading1"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="46" w:name="_Toc216505520"/><w:r><w:t>CHAPTER 4</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:br/><w:t>IMPLEMENTATION AND TESTING</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="46"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="56453B75" w14:textId="2CC067A3" w:rsidR="00D10183" w:rsidRDefault="00D10183" w:rsidP="00D10183"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="48" w:name="_Toc216505522"/><w:r><w:t>4.1.1 Tools Used</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="48"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="0DF8F76B" w14:textId="529C5F9B" Designing, coding and developing the application is done using open</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="001F787E"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>source and freely available tools to make the system flexible, secure and user</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="001F787E"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>friendly. It handles URL routing, server</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>side logic, templates, authentication and communication with the database. The framework helps implement the M.V.C pattern using models, views and templates and provides a secure admin panel for managing data.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="3193F03D" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="001F787E" w:rsidRPr="001F787E" w:rsidRDefault="001F787E" w:rsidP="001F787E"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:t>Python (ML, if you kept models): Python is also used for any machine learning logic such as donor response prediction or basic analytics, using libraries like NumPy and Pandas for data processing and model handling (only if these features are included in your final system).</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="1374131A" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="001F787E" w:rsidRPr="001F787E" w:rsidRDefault="001F787E" w:rsidP="001F787E"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t>Database</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="670D9619" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="001F787E" w:rsidRPr="001F787E" w:rsidRDefault="001F787E" w:rsidP="001F787E"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:t>SQLite: SQLite is used as the relational database for this project. It stores users, donors, patients, blood requests, blood inventory and transaction records. SQLite integrates directly with Django’s ORM, making migrations and queries simple and efficient for a college</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>level project.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="01245FE1" w14:textId="26465687" w:rsidR="006E024D" w:rsidRDefault="006E024D" w:rsidP="006E024D"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading2"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>4.1.2 Implementation Details of Modules</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="49"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="3C9FE381" w14:textId="03E9A924" w:rsidR="001F787E" w:rsidRPr="001F787E" w:rsidRDefault="00560405" w:rsidP="001F787E"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Blood Bank Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="001F787E" w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> is divided into several modules. Each module focuses on a specific part of the system and works together to support smooth blood bank operations.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="279AF80D" w14:textId="7072AA65" w:rsidR="00CA3EE0" w:rsidRDefault="001F787E" w:rsidP="000B5B68"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="center"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t>Registration Module</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00BF1715"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve">: </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:t>The registration module handles the logic for creating new users in the system. Patients and donors can register by filling in details such as name, email, phone number, address, blood group, username and password. After validation, the data is stored in SQLite through Django models, and the user account becomes active. On successful registration, users are redirected towards the login module for authentication.</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="000B5B68" w:rsidRPr="000B5B68"><w:rPr><w:noProof/></w:rPr><w:t w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t>Login Module</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00BF1715"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve">: </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="001F787E"><w:t>The login module is responsible for authenticating registered users and granting them access to the system. Users enter their username or email and password; the module verifies these credentials against the database using Django’s authentication system. Only valid users can log in and reach their respective dashboards. Invalid credentials show an error message and keep the user on the login page. Separate access levels are maintained for patients, donors and admin users.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="156F8378" w14:textId="41A5355E" w:rsidR="000B5B68" w:rsidRDefault="000B5B68" w:rsidR="00EA1979" w:rsidRPr="00C37BD3" w:rsidRDefault="00EA1979" w:rsidP="006178DF"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="center"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:cs="Times New w14:paraId="0CCA9EC8" w14:textId="543A2550" w:rsidR="004F7567" w:rsidRDefault="004F7567"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:after="160" w:line="278" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:br w:type="page"/></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="2D80AD6E" w14:textId="541FAA54" w:rsidR="0003624D" w:rsidRDefault="003827DA" w:rsidP="003827DA"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading2"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="60" w:name="_Toc216505527"/><w:r><w:t>4.3 Result Analysis</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="60"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="5A1D670B" w14:textId="307A7551" w:rsidR="0036138D" w:rsidRDefault="0036138D" w:rsidP="0036138D"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Accuracy is one of the most common metrics to evaluate the performance of a machine learning model. It represents the proportion of correctly classified instances out of all predictions. While accuracy provides an overall sense of model performance, it may be misleading for imbalanced datasets, where some classes occur much more frequently than others. Therefore, it should be considered alongside other metrics for a complete evaluation.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="69A4B4C9" w14:textId="29DD7912" w:rsidR="0036138D" w:rsidRDefault="0036138D" w:rsidP="0036138D"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Other key evaluation metrics include:</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="353ECE05" w14:textId="64F36CBB" w:rsidR="0036138D" w:rsidRDefault="0036138D" w:rsidP="0036138D"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Precision: Precision measures the ratio of correctly predicted positive instances to all instances predicted as positive. High precision indicates that the model makes few false positive errors. It is especially important when the cost of false positives is high. Precision focuses on the reliability of positive predictions rather than overall performance.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="52DEC9F1" w14:textId="699F26A3" w:rsidR="0036138D" w:rsidRDefault="0036138D" w:rsidP="0036138D"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Recall (Sensitivity): Recall measures the ratio of correctly predicted positive instances to all actual positive instances. High recall shows that the model successfully captures most of the actual positive cases. It is critical when missing positive cases is costly or undesirable. Recall emphasizes the model’s ability to detect rare or minority events.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="45FF760C" w14:textId="199A19DA" w:rsidR="0036138D" w:rsidRDefault="0036138D" w:rsidP="0036138D"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>F1-Score: The F1-score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single metric that balances both concerns. It is particularly useful when both false positives and false negatives matter. A high F1-score indicates the model is accurate and robust across different classes. This metric helps evaluate overall model effectiveness in imbalanced datasets.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="78B362A5" w14:textId="009FE9F6" w:rsidR="00A566AC" w:rsidRDefault="0036138D" w:rsidP="00A566AC"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Accuracy: Accuracy measures the ratio of correctly predicted instances to the total number of instances. It gives a general overview of model performance across all classes. However, in datasets with class imbalance, accuracy may be misleading as it can be dominated by the majority class. Combining accuracy with precision, recall, and F1-score provides a more complete assessment.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="11DF28CF" w14:textId="392D47A5" w:rsidR="0036138D" w:rsidRDefault="0036138D" w:rsidP="00A566AC"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="19225DFF" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="0036138D" w:rsidRPr="00A566AC" w:rsidRDefault="0036138D" w:rsidP="00A566AC"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="346E514E" w14:textId="14DC51AD" w:rsidR="00852FC0" w:rsidRDefault="00852FC0" w:rsidP="00852FC0"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading2"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="61" w:name="_Toc216505528"/><w:r><w:t>4.3.2 Evaluating Accuracy of the Models</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="61"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="219DA237" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00A566AC" w:rsidRPr="00A566AC" w:rsidRDefault="00A566AC" w:rsidP="00A566AC"><w:pPr><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00A566AC"><w:rPr><w:b/></w:rPr><w:t>Random Forest Model: Donor Response Prediction</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="63360587" w14:textId="77C0EABE" w:rsidR="00A566AC" w:rsidRPr="00A566AC" w:rsidRDefault="00A566AC" w:rsidP="00A566AC"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00A566AC"><w:t xml:space="preserve">The Random Forest model was designed to predict whether a donor will respond to a notification sent by the </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00560405"><w:t>Blood Bank Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidRPr="00A566AC"><w:t xml:space="preserve"> </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00A566AC"><w:t>system</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r w:rsidRPr="00A566AC"><w:t>. Confusion matrix of Manual Logistic Regression</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="66"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="417D3A42" w14:textId="02BD4365" w:rsidR="003E6079" w:rsidRDefault="00664947" w:rsidP="00664947"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>This figure is the confusion matrix for the Manual Logistic Regression model on the donation prediction task. The rows show the actual outcomes (“No Donation” and “Donated”) and the columns show the model’s predictions. The model correctly classifies 112 cases where donors did not donate, and makes only 1 false alarm by predicting “Donated” when there was actually no donation. However, among donors who truly donated, 36 are misclassified as “No Donation” and only 1 is correctly predicted as “Donated”. This pattern confirms that the model is heavily biased toward the majority “No Donation” class: it is very reliable at identifying non</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>donors but performs poorly at detecting donors who actually go on to donate.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="59F99E0B" w14:textId="489E0F5B" w:rsidR="00A3444B" w:rsidRDefault="00A3444B" w:rsidP="00C31E43"><w:pPr><w:spacing responders, but it sometimes over</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>predicts responses for donors who in reality do not reply.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="3E580F13" w14:textId="1732DEC1" w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="1E3FDF16" w14:textId="67832C36" w:rsidR="00E942C9" w:rsidRDefault="00E942C9" w:rsidP="00C31E43"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:val="28"/></w:rPr><w:t>Notification and Response Summary:</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="1AD28D3E" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00E942C9" w:rsidRDefault="00E942C9" w:rsidP="00E942C9"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/><w:rPr><w:szCs w:val="24"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>Total Notifications (118) shows how many donor–request matches the system generated and actually contacted. This is the top of the funnel: every later outcome (response and donation) depends on this pool. Total Responded (19, i.e., 16.10%) indicates that only about one in six notified donors interacted with the request at all (for example, opening the message and replying “willing”, “not available”, or similar). A response rate at this level suggests that many donors either do not see the notification, are not interested at that time, or face practical constraints such as schedule or distance.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="1A8142B1" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00E942C9" w:rsidRDefault="00E942C9" w:rsidP="00E942C9"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t>The most critical figure is Total Actually Donated: 0. Even though some donors responded, none progressed through appointment scheduling, eligibility checks, and final donation. This means the conversion from “interested” to “successful donation” is currently zero for this dataset. Practically, this reveals a large behavior gap: the prediction model may correctly identify donors likely to respond, but real</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>world factors—health conditions, last donation date, transport, hospital capacity, or lack of follow</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>up reminders—prevent those responses from turning into completed donations. For the system, this implies that improving operational workflows (e.g., clearer instructions, easier appointment booking, reminders, transport support, or pre</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>screening for eligibility) is just as important as improving the machine</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>learning model, because predictions alone are not enough if donors never reach the actual donation stage.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="2E440DC0" w14:textId="77777777" w:rsidR="00E942C9" w:rsidRPr="00E942C9" w:rsidRDefault="00E942C9" w14:paraId="334B53E5" w14:textId="6BDE4777" w:rsidR="008A4FBE" w:rsidRDefault="008A4FBE"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:after="160" w:line="278" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:br w:type="page"/></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="799D03C5" w14:textId="18AC9D14" w:rsidR="00715099" w:rsidRDefault="003A1F94" w:rsidP="003A1F94"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading1"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:id="68"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="3715358F" w14:textId="16E0A24D" w:rsidR="003A1F94" w:rsidRDefault="002328B3" w:rsidP="002328B3"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading2"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="69" w:name="_Toc216505530"/><w:r><w:t>5.1 Conclusion</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="69"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="717A53DA" w14:textId="2BB7C1FD" w:rsidR="00715BBF" w:rsidRDefault="007A3A2F" w:rsidP="007A3A2F"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">The </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00560405"><w:t>Blood Bank Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> </w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellStart"/><w:r><w:t>system</w:t></w:r><w:proofErr w:type="spellEnd"/><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> successfully models the end</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>to</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>end workflow of blood requests and donations using a well</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>structured set of classes (patients, donors, admins, notifications, appointments, requests, inventory, and issuances) and clearly defined interactions between them. The UML class, activity, sequence, component, and deployment diagrams together show that the system supports all key processes: registering users, creating and approving blood requests, matching donors by blood type and location, scheduling donation appointments, and updating blood inventory and issuance records. The machine</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>learning experiments demonstrate that both Logistic Regression and Random Forest can distinguish between donors who respond and those who do not, with Random Forest generally offering better discriminative power (higher AUC and better detection of responders), although both models are affected by class imbalance and limited donation conversions. Overall, the prototype proves that integrating spatial data, notification mechanisms, and predictive models into a unified web platform is technically feasible and can provide structured support for hospitals in managing emergency and routine blood requirements.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="134413BD" w14:textId="25CA3649" w:rsidR="007418EE" w:rsidRDefault="007418EE" w:rsidP="007418EE"><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val="Heading2"/><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:bookmarkStart w:id="70" w:name="_Toc216505531"/><w:r><w:t>5.2 Future Recommendations</w:t></w:r><w:bookmarkEnd w:id="70"/></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="0B306837" w14:textId="7C5C5074" w:rsidR="00ED1FBD" w:rsidRDefault="007A3A2F" w:rsidP="007A3A2F"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:line="360" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:jc w:val="both"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve">Future work on </w:t></w:r><w:r w:rsidR="00560405"><w:t>Blood Bank Management System</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:t xml:space="preserve"> should focus on both improving the predictive models and strengthening the operational workflow around them. The training data needs to be expanded and balanced, especially for the “responded” and “donated” classes, so that models are less biased toward non</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>response and can learn more reliable patterns. In parallel, the system should introduce features that make it easier for interested donors to complete donations, such as clearer eligibility checks, streamlined appointment booking, automated reminders, and flexible rescheduling options. Adding real</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>time dashboards for admins to monitor requests, inventory levels, and donor</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>response funnels would support better decision</w:t></w:r><w:r><w:noBreakHyphen/><w:t>making, while gradual enhancements to security, privacy controls, and system scalability would prepare the platform for deployment across multiple hospitals and regions.</w:t></w:r></w:p><w:p w14:paraId="0B2CEB56" w14:textId="0FE1A7D7" w:rsidR="00372E94" w:rsidRDefault="00372E94"><w:pPr><w:spacing w:after="160" w:line="278" w:lineRule="auto"/></w:pPr><w:r><w:br w:type="page"/></w:r></w:p><w:bookmarkStart w:id="71" w:name="_Toc216505532" w:displacedByCustomXml="next"/><w:sdt><w:sdtPr><w:rPr><w:rFonts
